1.author: Ahmed Ali
1.text: NUN. I CALL to witness the pen and what they inscribe,

1.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
1.text: Nuun* – by oath of the pen and by oath of what is written by it. (Alphabet of the Arabic language; Allah and to whomever He reveals, know their precise meanings.)

1.author: Arberry
1.text: Nun. By the Pen, and what they inscribe,

1.author: Daryabadi
1.text: Nun By the pen and by that which they inscribe.

1.author: Hilali & Khan
1.text: Nun. [These letters (Nun, etc.) are one of the miracles of the Quran, and none but Allah (Alone) knows their meanings]. By the pen and what the (angels) write (in the Records of men).

1.author: Itani
1.text: Noon. By the pen, and by what they inscribe.

1.author: Maududi
1.text: Nun. By the pen and what the scribes write.

1.author: Mubarakpuri
1.text: Nun. By the pen and by what they Yastur.

1.author: Pickthall
1.text: Nun. By the pen and that which they write (therewith),

1.author: Qarai
1.text: Nun. By the Pen and what they write:

1.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
1.text: Noon. By the Pen and that (the angels) write,

1.author: Saheeh International
1.text: Nun. By the pen and what they inscribe,

1.author: Sarwar
1.text: Nun. By the pen and by what you write,

1.author: Shakir
1.text: Noon. I swear by the pen and what the angels write,

1.author: Wahiduddin Khan
1.text: Nun. By the pen, and all that they write!

1.author: Yusuf Ali
1.text: Nun. By the Pen and the (Record) which (men) write,-

2.author: Ahmed Ali
2.text: You are not demented by the grace of your Lord.

2.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
2.text: You are not insane, by the munificence of your Lord.

2.author: Arberry
2.text: thou art not, by the blessing of thy Lord, a man possessed.

2.author: Daryabadi
2.text: Thou art nor, through the grace of thy Lord, mad.

2.author: Hilali & Khan
2.text: You (O Muhammad SAW) are not, by the Grace of your Lord, a madman.

2.author: Itani
2.text: By the grace of your Lord, you are not insane.

2.author: Maududi
2.text: By your Lord's Grace, you are not afflicted with madness,

2.author: Mubarakpuri
2.text: You, by the grace of your Lord, are not insane.

2.author: Pickthall
2.text: Thou art not, for thy Lord's favour unto thee, a madman.

2.author: Qarai
2.text: by your Lord’s blessing, you are not, crazy,

2.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
2.text: you are not, because of the favor of your Lord, mad.

2.author: Saheeh International
2.text: You are not, [O Muhammad], by the favor of your Lord, a madman.

2.author: Sarwar
2.text: (Muhammad), you are not insane, thanks to the bounty of your Lord.

2.author: Shakir
2.text: By the grace of your Lord you are not mad.

2.author: Wahiduddin Khan
2.text: By the grace of your Lord, you are not a mad man.

2.author: Yusuf Ali
2.text: Thou art not, by the Grace of thy Lord, mad or possessed.

3.author: Ahmed Ali
3.text: There is surely reward unending for you,

3.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
3.text: And indeed for you is an unlimited reward.

3.author: Arberry
3.text: Surely thou shalt have a wage unfailing;

3.author: Daryabadi
3.text: And verily thine shall be a hire unending."

3.author: Hilali & Khan
3.text: And verily, for you (O Muhammad SAW) will be an endless reward.

3.author: Itani
3.text: In fact, you will have a reward that will never end.

3.author: Maududi
3.text: and surely yours shall be a never-ending reward,

3.author: Mubarakpuri
3.text: And verily, for you will be reward that is not Mamnun.

3.author: Pickthall
3.text: And lo! thine verily will be a reward unfailing.

3.author: Qarai
3.text: and yours indeed will be an everlasting reward,

3.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
3.text: Indeed, there is an unfailing wage for you.

3.author: Saheeh International
3.text: And indeed, for you is a reward uninterrupted.

3.author: Sarwar
3.text: You will certainly receive a never-ending reward.

3.author: Shakir
3.text: And most surely you shall have a reward never to be cut off.

3.author: Wahiduddin Khan
3.text: Most surely, you will have a never ending reward.

3.author: Yusuf Ali
3.text: Nay, verily for thee is a Reward unfailing:

4.author: Ahmed Ali
4.text: For you are verily born of sublime nature.

4.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
4.text: And indeed you possess an exemplary character.

4.author: Arberry
4.text: surely thou art upon a mighty morality.

4.author: Daryabadi
4.text: And verily thou art of a high and noble disposition.

4.author: Hilali & Khan
4.text: And verily, you (O Muhammad SAW) are on an exalted standard of character.

4.author: Itani
4.text: And you are of a great moral character.

4.author: Maududi
4.text: and you are certainly on the most exalted standard of moral excellence.

4.author: Mubarakpuri
4.text: And verily, you are on an exalted character.

4.author: Pickthall
4.text: And lo! thou art of a tremendous nature.

4.author: Qarai
4.text: and indeed you possess a great character.

4.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
4.text: Surely, you (Prophet Muhammad) are of a great morality.

4.author: Saheeh International
4.text: And indeed, you are of a great moral character.

4.author: Sarwar
4.text: You have attained a high moral standard.

4.author: Shakir
4.text: And most surely you conform (yourself) to sublime morality.

4.author: Wahiduddin Khan
4.text: For you are truly of a sublime character.

4.author: Yusuf Ali
4.text: And thou (standest) on an exalted standard of character.

5.author: Ahmed Ali
5.text: So you will see, and they will realise,

5.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
5.text: So very soon, you will see and they too will realise –

5.author: Arberry
5.text: So thou shalt see, and they will see,

5.author: Daryabadi
5.text: Anon thou wilt see and they will see:

5.author: Hilali & Khan
5.text: You will see, and they will see,

5.author: Itani
5.text: You will see, and they will see.

5.author: Maududi
5.text: So you will soon see, and they too will see,

5.author: Mubarakpuri
5.text: You will see, and they will see,

5.author: Pickthall
5.text: And thou wilt see and they will see

5.author: Qarai
5.text: You will see and they will see,

5.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
5.text: You shall see and they will see

5.author: Saheeh International
5.text: So you will see and they will see

5.author: Sarwar
5.text: You will see and they will also see

5.author: Shakir
5.text: So you shall see, and they (too) shall see,

5.author: Wahiduddin Khan
5.text: Soon you will see, as will they,

5.author: Yusuf Ali
5.text: Soon wilt thou see, and they will see,

6.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
6.text: - That who among you was insane.

6.author: Arberry
6.text: which of you is the demented.

6.author: Daryabadi
6.text: Which of you is afflicted with madness.

6.author: Hilali & Khan
6.text: Which of you is afflicted with madness.

6.author: Itani
6.text: Which of you is the afflicted.

6.author: Maududi
6.text: which of you is afflicted with madness.

6.author: Mubarakpuri
6.text: Which of you is afflicted with madness.

6.author: Pickthall
6.text: Which of you is the demented.

6.author: Qarai
6.text: which one of you is crazy.

6.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
6.text: which of you is the demented.

6.author: Saheeh International
6.text: Which of you is the afflicted [by a devil].

6.author: Sarwar
6.text: which of you has been afflicted by insanity.

6.author: Shakir
6.text: Which of you is afflicted with madness.

6.author: Wahiduddin Khan
6.text: which of you is a prey to madness.

6.author: Yusuf Ali
6.text: Which of you is afflicted with madness.

7.author: Ahmed Ali
7.text: Verily your Lord knows those who have gone astray from His path, and He knows those who are guided on the way.

7.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
7.text: Indeed your Lord well knows those who have strayed from His path, and He well knows those who are upon guidance.

7.author: Arberry
7.text: Surely thy Lord knows very well those who have gone astray from His way, and He knows very well those who are guided.

7.author: Daryabadi
7.text: Verily thy Lord! He is the best Knower of him who strayeth from His path, and He is the best Knower of the guided one,

7.author: Hilali & Khan
7.text: Verily, your Lord knows better, who (among men) has gone astray from His Path, and He knows better those who are guided.

7.author: Itani
7.text: Your Lord knows best who has strayed from His path, and He knows best the well-guided.

7.author: Maududi
7.text: Surely your Lord knows well those who have strayed from His Way just as He knows well those who are on the Right Way.

7.author: Mubarakpuri
7.text: Verily, your Lord is the best Knower of him who has gone astray from His path, and He is the best Knower of those who are guided.

7.author: Pickthall
7.text: Lo! thy Lord is Best Aware of him who strayeth from His way, and He is Best Aware of those who walk aright.

7.author: Qarai
7.text: Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided.

7.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
7.text: Indeed, your Lord knows very well those who strayed from His Path, and those who are guided.

7.author: Saheeh International
7.text: Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who has gone astray from His way, and He is most knowing of the [rightly] guided.

7.author: Sarwar
7.text: Your Lord knows best who has gone astray from His path and who is rightly guided.

7.author: Shakir
7.text: Surely your Lord best knows him who errs from His way, and He best knows the followers of the right course.

7.author: Wahiduddin Khan
7.text: Your Lord knows best who has fallen by the wayside, and who has remained on the true path.

7.author: Yusuf Ali
7.text: Verily it is thy Lord that knoweth best, which (among men) hath strayed from His Path: and He knoweth best those who receive (true) Guidance.

7.author: Transliteration
7.text: Inna rabbaka huwa aAAlamu biman dalla AAan sabeelihi wahuwa aAAlamu bialmuhtadeena

8.author: Ahmed Ali
8.text: So do not comply with those who deny:

8.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
8.text: Therefore do not listen to the deniers.

8.author: Arberry
8.text: So obey thou not those who cry lies.

8.author: Daryabadi
8.text: Wherefore obey not thou the beliers.

8.author: Hilali & Khan
8.text: So (O Muhammad SAW) obey not the deniers [(of Islamic Monotheism those who belie the Verses of Allah), the Oneness of Allah, and the Messenger of Allah (Muhammad SAW), etc.]

8.author: Itani
8.text: So do not obey the deniers.

8.author: Maududi
8.text: Do not, then, yield to those who reject the Truth, decrying it as false;

8.author: Mubarakpuri
8.text: So, do not obey the deniers.

8.author: Pickthall
8.text: Therefor obey not thou the rejecters

8.author: Qarai
8.text: So do not obey the deniers,

8.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
8.text: Therefore, do not obey those who belie,

8.author: Saheeh International
8.text: Then do not obey the deniers.

8.author: Sarwar
8.text: Do not yield to those who reject the Truth.

8.author: Shakir
8.text: So do not yield to the rejecters.

8.author: Wahiduddin Khan
8.text: Do not give in to the deniers of truth.

8.author: Yusuf Ali
8.text: So hearken not to those who deny (the Truth).

9.author: Ahmed Ali
9.text: They only want that you should relent, so that they may come to terms.

9.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
9.text: They wish that in some way you may yield, so they too might soften their stand.

9.author: Arberry
9.text: They wish that thou shouldst compromise, then they would compromise.

9.author: Daryabadi
9.text: Fain would they that thou shouldst be pliant, so that they will be pliant.

9.author: Hilali & Khan
9.text: They wish that you should compromise (in religion out of courtesy) with them, so they (too) would compromise with you.

9.author: Itani
9.text: They would like you to compromise, so they would compromise.

9.author: Maududi
9.text: they would wish you to be pliant so that they too may be pliant.

9.author: Mubarakpuri
9.text: They wish that you should compromise with them, so they (too) would compromise with you.

9.author: Pickthall
9.text: Who would have had thee compromise, that they may compromise.

9.author: Qarai
9.text: who are eager that you should be flexible, so that they [too] may be flexible [towards you].

9.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
9.text: they wish you would compromise, then, they would compromise.

9.author: Saheeh International
9.text: They wish that you would soften [in your position], so they would soften [toward you].

9.author: Sarwar
9.text: They would like you to relent to them so that they could also relent towards you.

9.author: Shakir
9.text: They wish that you should be pliant so they (too) would be pliant.

9.author: Wahiduddin Khan
9.text: They want you to make concessions to them and then they will reciprocate.

9.author: Yusuf Ali
9.text: Their desire is that thou shouldst be pliant: so would they be pliant.

10.author: Ahmed Ali
10.text: Do not heed a contemptible swearer,

10.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
10.text: Nor ever listen to any excessive oath maker, ignoble person.

10.author: Arberry
10.text: And obey thou not every mean swearer,

10.author: Daryabadi
10.text: And obey not: thou any swearer ignominous.

10.author: Hilali & Khan
10.text: And obey not everyone who swears much, and is considered worthless,

10.author: Itani
10.text: And do not obey any vile swearer.

10.author: Maududi
10.text: And do not yield to any contemptible swearer,

10.author: Mubarakpuri
10.text: And do not obey every Hallaf Mahin.

10.author: Pickthall
10.text: Neither obey thou each feeble oath-monger,

10.author: Qarai
10.text: And do not obey any vile swearer,

10.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
10.text: And do not obey every mean swearer,

10.author: Saheeh International
10.text: And do not obey every worthless habitual swearer

10.author: Sarwar
10.text: Do not yield to one persistent in swearing,

10.author: Shakir
10.text: And yield not to any mean swearer

10.author: Wahiduddin Khan
10.text: Do not yield to any contemptible swearer of oaths,

10.author: Yusuf Ali
10.text: Heed not the type of despicable men,- ready with oaths,

11.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
11.text: The excessively insulting one, spreader of spite.

11.author: Daryabadi
11.text: A defamer, spreader abroad of slander.

11.author: Hilali & Khan
11.text: A slanderer, going about with calumnies,

11.author: Maududi
11.text: the fault-finder who goes around slandering,

11.author: Mubarakpuri
11.text: A Hammaz, going about with Namim,

11.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
11.text: the backbiter who goes about slandering,

11.author: Saheeh International
11.text: [And] scorner, going about with malicious gossip -

11.author: Wahiduddin Khan
11.text: or to any defamer or one who spreads slander,

11.author: Yusuf Ali
11.text: A slanderer, going about with calumnies,

12.author: Ahmed Ali
12.text: Who hinders men from (doing) good, the transgressor, the iniquitous,

12.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
12.text: One who excessively forbids the good, transgressor, sinner.

12.author: Arberry
12.text: hinderer of good, guilty aggressor, coarse-grained,

12.author: Daryabadi
12.text: Hinderer of the good, trespasser; sinner.

12.author: Hilali & Khan
12.text: Hinderer of the good, transgressor, sinful,

12.author: Maududi
12.text: the hinderer of good, the transgressor, the sinful;

12.author: Mubarakpuri
12.text: Hinderer of the good, transgressor, sinful,

12.author: Pickthall
12.text: Hinderer of the good, transgressor, malefactor

12.author: Qarai
12.text: hinderer of all good, sinful transgressor,

12.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
12.text: those who hinder good, the guilty aggressor,

12.author: Saheeh International
12.text: A preventer of good, transgressing and sinful,

12.author: Shakir
12.text: Forbidder of good, outstepping the limits, sinful,

12.author: Wahiduddin Khan
12.text: or to one who places obstacles in the way of good being done or to the wicked transgressor,

12.author: Yusuf Ali
12.text: (Habitually) hindering (all) good, transgressing beyond bounds, deep in sin,

13.author: Ahmed Ali
13.text: Crude, and above all, mean and infamous,

13.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
13.text: Foul mouthed, and in addition to all this, of improper lineage.

13.author: Hilali & Khan
13.text: Cruel, after all that base-born (of illegitimate birth),

13.author: Maududi
13.text: the coarse-grained, and above all mean and ignoble;

13.author: Qarai
13.text: callous and, on top of that, baseborn

13.author: Saheeh International
13.text: Cruel, moreover, and an illegitimate pretender.

13.author: Sarwar
13.text: ill-mannered, and moraly corrupt or that because he may possess wealth and children.

13.author: Wahiduddin Khan
13.text: who is ignoble and besides all that, base-born;

13.author: Yusuf Ali
13.text: Violent (and cruel),- with all that, base-born,-

14.author: Ahmed Ali
14.text: Simply because he possesses wealth and children.

14.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
14.text: Because he* has some wealth and sons. (Walid bin Mugaira, who cursed the Holy Prophet.)

14.author: Arberry
14.text: because he has wealth and sons.

14.author: Daryabadi
14.text: Because he is owner of riches and children.

14.author: Hilali & Khan
14.text: (He was so) because he had wealth and children.

14.author: Itani
14.text: Just because he has money and children.

14.author: Maududi
14.text: (who so acts) simply because he has wealth and sons,

14.author: Mubarakpuri
14.text: (He was so) because he had wealth and children.

14.author: Pickthall
14.text: It is because he is possessed of wealth and children

14.author: Qarai
14.text: —[who behaves in such a manner only] because he has wealth and children.

14.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
14.text: because he has wealth and sons.

14.author: Saheeh International
14.text: Because he is a possessor of wealth and children,

14.author: Sarwar
14.text: When Our revelations are recited to him,

14.author: Shakir
14.text: Because he possesses wealth and sons.

14.author: Wahiduddin Khan
14.text: just because he has wealth and sons,

14.author: Yusuf Ali
14.text: Because he possesses wealth and (numerous) sons.

15.author: Ahmed Ali
15.text: When you recite Our revelations to him, he says: "These are fables of long ago."

15.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
15.text: When Our verses are recited to him, he says, “These are stories of earlier people.”

15.author: Arberry
15.text: When Our signs are recited to him, he says, 'Fairy-tales of the ancients!'

15.author: Daryabadi
15.text: When Our revelations are rehearsed Unto him, he saith: fables of the ancients.

15.author: Hilali & Khan
15.text: When Our Verses (of the Quran) are recited to him, he says: "Tales of the men of old!"

15.author: Itani
15.text: When Our Verses are recited to him, he says, “Myths of the ancients!”

15.author: Maududi
15.text: and whenever Our verses are rehearsed to him, he says: “These are fairy- tales of times gone by.”

15.author: Mubarakpuri
15.text: When Our Ayat are recited to him, he says: "Tales of the men of old!"

15.author: Pickthall
15.text: That, when Our revelations are recited unto him, he saith: Mere fables of the men of old.

15.author: Qarai
15.text: When Our signs are recited to him, he says, ‘Myths of the ancients!’

15.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
15.text: When Our verses are recited to him, he says: 'They are but fairytales of the ancients'

15.author: Saheeh International
15.text: When Our verses are recited to him, he says, "Legends of the former peoples."

15.author: Shakir
15.text: When Our communications are recited to him, he says: Stories of those of yore.

15.author: Wahiduddin Khan
15.text: when Our revelations are recited to him, he says, "These are just ancient fables."

15.author: Yusuf Ali
15.text: When to him are rehearsed Our Signs, "Tales of the ancients", he cries!

15.author: Transliteration
15.text: Itha tutla AAalayhi ayatuna qala asateeru alawwaleena

16.author: Ahmed Ali
16.text: We shall brand him on the muzzle.

16.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
16.text: We will soon singe his pig-nose.

16.author: Arberry
16.text: We shall brand him upon the muzzle!

16.author: Daryabadi
16.text: Anon We shall brand him on snout.

16.author: Hilali & Khan
16.text: We shall brand him over the nose!

16.author: Itani
16.text: We will brand him on the muzzle.

16.author: Maududi
16.text: Soon shall We brand him on his snout.

16.author: Mubarakpuri
16.text: We shall brand him on the snout (nose)!

16.author: Pickthall
16.text: We shall brand him on the nose.

16.author: Qarai
16.text: Soon We shall brand him on his snout.

16.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
16.text: We shall mark him upon his nose!

16.author: Saheeh International
16.text: We will brand him upon the snout.

16.author: Sarwar
16.text: We shall brand him on his nose.

16.author: Shakir
16.text: We will brand him on the nose.

16.author: Wahiduddin Khan
16.text: Soon We will brand him on the nose.

16.author: Yusuf Ali
16.text: Soon shall We brand (the beast) on the snout!

17.author: Ahmed Ali
17.text: We have tried you as We tried the owners of the garden when they vowed to gather the fruits in the morning

17.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
17.text: We have indeed tested them the way We had tested the owners of the garden when they swore that they would reap its harvest the next morning.

17.author: Arberry
17.text: Now We have tried them, even as We tried the owners of the garden when they swore they would pluck in the morning

17.author: Daryabadi
17.text: Verily We! We have proved them even as We proved the fellows of a garden when they swarethat they would surely reap it in the morning.

17.author: Hilali & Khan
17.text: Verily, We have tried them as We tried the people of the garden, when they swore to pluck the fruits of the (garden) in the morning,

17.author: Itani
17.text: We tested them, as We tested the owners of the garden, when they vowed to harvest it in the morning.

17.author: Maududi
17.text: We have put them [i.e., the Makkans] to test even as We put to test the owners of the orchard when they vowed that they would gather the fruit of their orchard in the morning,

17.author: Mubarakpuri
17.text: Verily, We have tried them as We tried the People of the Garden, when they swore to pluck the fruits in the morning,

17.author: Pickthall
17.text: Lo! We have tried them as We tried the owners of the garden when they vowed that they would pluck its fruit next morning,

17.author: Qarai
17.text: Indeed we have tested them just as We tested the People of the Garden when they vowed they would gather its fruit at dawn,

17.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
17.text: We have tried them as We tried the owners of the garden who had sworn that in the morning they would reap it,

17.author: Saheeh International
17.text: Indeed, We have tried them as We tried the companions of the garden, when they swore to cut its fruit in the [early] morning

17.author: Sarwar
17.text: We have tested them in the same way as we tested the dwellers of the garden (in Yemen) when they swore to pluck all the fruits of the garden in the morning,

17.author: Shakir
17.text: Surely We will try them as We tried the owners of the garden, when they swore that they would certainly cut off the produce in the morning,

17.author: Wahiduddin Khan
17.text: We have tried them as we tried the owners of a certain orchard, who vowed to harvest all its fruits the next morning,

17.author: Yusuf Ali
17.text: Verily We have tried them as We tried the People of the Garden, when they resolved to gather the fruits of the (garden) in the morning.

17.author: Transliteration
17.text: Inna balawnahum kama balawna ashaba aljannati ith aqsamoo layasrimunnaha musbiheena

18.author: Ahmed Ali
18.text: But did not add: "If God may please."

18.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
18.text: And they did not say, “If Allah wills”.

18.author: Arberry
18.text: and they added not the saving words.

18.author: Daryabadi
18.text: And they made not the exception.

18.author: Hilali & Khan
18.text: Without saying: Insha' Allah (If Allah will).

18.author: Maududi
18.text: without making any allowance (for the will of Allah).

18.author: Pickthall
18.text: And made no exception (for the Will of Allah);

18.author: Qarai
18.text: and they did not make any exception.

18.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
18.text: without adding to their swearing, (Allah willing, InshaAllah).

18.author: Shakir
18.text: And were not willing to set aside a portion (for the poor).

18.author: Wahiduddin Khan
18.text: without saying, "If it be God's will."

18.author: Yusuf Ali
18.text: But made no reservation, ("If it be Allah's Will").

19.author: Ahmed Ali
19.text: Then a calamity from your Lord fell upon it, but they remained fast asleep.

19.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
19.text: So an envoy from your Lord completed his round upon the garden, whilst they were sleeping.

19.author: Arberry
19.text: Then a visitation from thy Lord visited it, while they were sleeping,

19.author: Daryabadi
19.text: Wherefore an encircling visitation visited it even as they slept on.

19.author: Hilali & Khan
19.text: Then there passed by on the (garden) something (fire) from your Lord at night and burnt it while they were asleep.

19.author: Itani
19.text: But a calamity from your Lord went around it while they slept.

19.author: Maududi
19.text: Thereupon a calamity from your Lord passed over it while they were asleep,

19.author: Mubarakpuri
19.text: Then there passed over it a Ta'if from your Lord while they were asleep.

19.author: Pickthall
19.text: Then a visitation from thy Lord came upon it while they slept

19.author: Qarai
19.text: Then, a visitation from your Lord visited it while they were asleep.

19.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
19.text: Then, a visitation from your Lord came down upon it while they slept,

19.author: Saheeh International
19.text: So there came upon the garden an affliction from your Lord while they were asleep.

19.author: Sarwar
19.text: A visitor from your Lord circled around the garden during the night while they were asleep

19.author: Shakir
19.text: Then there encompassed it a visitation from your Lord while they were sleeping.

19.author: Wahiduddin Khan
19.text: A calamity from your Lord befell the orchard as they slept.

19.author: Yusuf Ali
19.text: Then there came on the (garden) a visitation from thy Lord, (which swept away) all around, while they were asleep.

19.author: Transliteration
19.text: Fatafa AAalayha taifun min rabbika wahum naimoona

20.author: Ahmed Ali
20.text: So by the morning it seemed as though picked clean.

20.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
20.text: So in the morning it became as if harvested.

20.author: Arberry
20.text: and in the morning it was as if it were a garden plucked.

20.author: Daryabadi
20.text: Then in the morning it became as though it had been reaped.

20.author: Hilali & Khan
20.text: So the (garden) became black by the morning, like a pitch dark night (in complete ruins).

20.author: Itani
20.text: And in the morning it was as if picked.

20.author: Maududi
20.text: and so by morning the orchard lay as though it had been fully harvested.

20.author: Mubarakpuri
20.text: So by the morning, it became like As-Sarim.

20.author: Pickthall
20.text: And in the morning it was as if plucked.

20.author: Qarai
20.text: So, by dawn it was like a harvested field.

20.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
20.text: and in the morning it was if the garden had been reaped.

20.author: Saheeh International
20.text: And it became as though reaped.

20.author: Sarwar
20.text: and the garden was turned into a barren desert.

20.author: Shakir
20.text: So it became as black, barren land.

20.author: Wahiduddin Khan
20.text: And by morning it lay as if it had already been harvested, a barren land.

20.author: Yusuf Ali
20.text: So the (garden) became, by the morning, like a dark and desolate spot, (whose fruit had been gathered).

21.author: Ahmed Ali
21.text: At daybreak they called to each other:

21.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
21.text: They then called out to each other at daybreak.

21.author: Arberry
21.text: In the morning they called to one another,

21.author: Daryabadi
21.text: Then they cried out Unto each other in the morning,

21.author: Hilali & Khan
21.text: Then they called out one to another as soon as the morning broke,

21.author: Itani
21.text: In the morning, they called to one another.

21.author: Maududi
21.text: At daybreak they called out to one another:

21.author: Mubarakpuri
21.text: Then they called out (one to another as soon) as the morning broke.

21.author: Pickthall
21.text: And they cried out one unto another in the morning,

21.author: Qarai
21.text: At dawn they called out to one another,

21.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
21.text: (Then) in the morning they called out to one another, saying:

21.author: Saheeh International
21.text: And they called one another at morning,

21.author: Sarwar
21.text: In the morning they called out to one another,

21.author: Shakir
21.text: And they called out to each other in the morning,

21.author: Wahiduddin Khan
21.text: So they called out to each other at the break of dawn,

21.author: Yusuf Ali
21.text: As the morning broke, they called out, one to another,-

22.author: Ahmed Ali
22.text: "If you want to gather the fruits, let us go early to the plantation."

22.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
22.text: That, “Go to your fields at early morn, if you want to harvest.”

22.author: Arberry
22.text: 'Come forth betimes upon your tillage, if you would pluck!'

22.author: Daryabadi
22.text: Saying: go out betimes to your tilth if ye would reap.

22.author: Hilali & Khan
22.text: Saying: "Go to your tilth in the morning, if you would pluck the fruits."

22.author: Itani
22.text: “Go early to your plantation, if you are going to harvest.”

22.author: Maududi
22.text: “Hurry to your orchard if you would gather its fruit.”

22.author: Mubarakpuri
22.text: Saying: "Go to your tilth in the morning, if you would pluck (the fruits)."

22.author: Pickthall
22.text: Saying: Run unto your field if ye would pluck (the fruit).

22.author: Qarai
22.text: ‘Get off early to your field if you have to gather [the fruits].’

22.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
22.text: 'Come out to your tillage if you want to reap'

22.author: Saheeh International
22.text: [Saying], "Go early to your crop if you would cut the fruit."

22.author: Sarwar
22.text: "Go early to your farms, if you want to pluck the fruits".

22.author: Shakir
22.text: Saying: Go early to your tilth if you would cut (the produce).

22.author: Wahiduddin Khan
22.text: saying, "Be quick to reach your orchard, if you want to gather all your fruits."

22.author: Yusuf Ali
22.text: "Go ye to your tilth (betimes) in the morning, if ye would gather the fruits."

22.author: Transliteration
22.text: Ani ighdoo AAala harthikum in kuntum sarimeena

23.author: Ahmed Ali
23.text: So they departed, talking in low voices:

23.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
23.text: So they went off, while whispering to one another.

23.author: Daryabadi
23.text: So they went off speaking to each other in a low voice:

23.author: Hilali & Khan
23.text: So they departed, conversing in secret low tones (saying),

23.author: Itani
23.text: So off they went, murmuring to one another.

23.author: Maududi
23.text: So off they went, whispering to one another:

23.author: Mubarakpuri
23.text: So they departed, and they were whispering:

23.author: Pickthall
23.text: So they went off, saying one unto another in low tones:

23.author: Qarai
23.text: So off they went, murmuring to one another:

23.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
23.text: And so they departed, whispering to one another:

23.author: Saheeh International
23.text: So they set out, while lowering their voices,

23.author: Sarwar
23.text: They all left, whispering to one another,

23.author: Shakir
23.text: So they went, while they consulted together secretly,

23.author: Wahiduddin Khan
23.text: So they went off, whispering to one another,

23.author: Yusuf Ali
23.text: So they departed, conversing in secret low tones, (saying)-

24.author: Ahmed Ali
24.text: "Let no needy person come to you within it today."

24.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
24.text: “Make sure that no needy person enters your garden this day.”

24.author: Arberry
24.text: 'No needy man shall enter it today against your will.'

24.author: Daryabadi
24.text: Let there not enter upon you today any needy man.

24.author: Hilali & Khan
24.text: No Miskin (poor man) shall enter upon you into it today.

24.author: Itani
24.text: “No poor person is to enter it upon you today.”

24.author: Maududi
24.text: “No destitute person shall enter it today.”

24.author: Mubarakpuri
24.text: "No poor person shall enter upon you into it today."

24.author: Pickthall
24.text: No needy man shall enter it to-day against you.

24.author: Qarai
24.text: ‘Today no needy man shall come to you in it.’

24.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
24.text: 'No needy person shall set foot in it today'

24.author: Saheeh International
24.text: [Saying], "There will surely not enter it today upon you [any] poor person."

24.author: Sarwar
24.text: "Let no beggar come to the garden".

24.author: Shakir
24.text: Saying: No poor man shall enter it today upon you.

24.author: Wahiduddin Khan
24.text: "Be sure to stop any poor person from entering the orchard today."

24.author: Yusuf Ali
24.text: "Let not a single indigent person break in upon you into the (garden) this day."

24.author: Transliteration
24.text: An la yadkhulannaha alyawma AAalaykum miskeenun

25.author: Ahmed Ali
25.text: They left early in the morning bent on this purpose.

25.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
25.text: And they left at early morn, assuming they were in control of their purpose.

25.author: Arberry
25.text: And they went forth early, determined upon their purpose.

25.author: Daryabadi
25.text: And they went out betimes determined in purpose.

25.author: Hilali & Khan
25.text: And they went in the morning with strong intention, thinking that they have power (to prevent the poor taking anything of the fruits therefrom).

25.author: Itani
25.text: And early they went, resolved in intent.

25.author: Maududi
25.text: They went forth early, believing that they had the power (to gather the fruit).

25.author: Mubarakpuri
25.text: And they went in the morning with Hard Qadirin.

25.author: Pickthall
25.text: They went betimes, strong in (this) purpose.

25.author: Qarai
25.text: And they set out at early morning, [considering themselves] able to deprive [the poor of its fruit].

25.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
25.text: And they went out early, determined in their resolve.

25.author: Saheeh International
25.text: And they went early in determination, [assuming themselves] able.

25.author: Sarwar
25.text: They were resolved to repel the beggars.

25.author: Shakir
25.text: And in the morning they went, having the power to prevent.

25.author: Wahiduddin Khan
25.text: They set out early in the morning, thinking they had the power to prevent.

25.author: Yusuf Ali
25.text: And they opened the morning, strong in an (unjust) resolve.

26.author: Ahmed Ali
26.text: When they saw (and did not recognise it) they said: "Surely we have lost the way.

26.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
26.text: Then when they saw it, they said, “We have indeed strayed.”

26.author: Arberry
26.text: But when they saw it, they said, 'Surely we are gone astray;

26.author: Daryabadi
26.text: Then when they beheld it, they said: verily we have strayed.

26.author: Hilali & Khan
26.text: But when they saw the (garden), they said: "Verily, we have gone astray,"

26.author: Itani
26.text: But when they saw it, they said, “We were wrong.

26.author: Maududi
26.text: But as soon as they beheld the orchard, (they cried out): “We have certainly lost the way;

26.author: Mubarakpuri
26.text: But when they saw it, they said: "Verily, we have gone astray."

26.author: Pickthall
26.text: But when they saw it, they said: Lo! we are in error!

26.author: Qarai
26.text: But when they saw it, they said, ‘We have indeed lost our way!’

26.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
26.text: But when they saw it they said: 'We have surely gone astray.

26.author: Saheeh International
26.text: But when they saw it, they said, "Indeed, we are lost;

26.author: Sarwar
26.text: When they saw the garden, they said, "Surely we have lost our way.

26.author: Shakir
26.text: But when they saw it, they said: Most surely we have gone astray

26.author: Wahiduddin Khan
26.text: But when they saw it, they said, "We must have lost our way.

26.author: Yusuf Ali
26.text: But when they saw the (garden), they said: "We have surely lost our way:

27.author: Ahmed Ali
27.text: No. In fact we have been deprived of it."

27.author: Arberry
27.text: nay, rather we have been robbed!'

27.author: Daryabadi
27.text: Aye! it is we who are deprived!

27.author: Hilali & Khan
27.text: (Then they said): "Nay! Indeed we are deprived of (the fruits)!"

27.author: Qarai
27.text: ‘No, it is we who have been deprived!’

27.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
27.text: No, rather, we have been prevented'

27.author: Sarwar
27.text: (No, we are not lost.) In fact, we have been deprived of everything".

27.author: Shakir
27.text: Nay! we are made to suffer privation.

27.author: Yusuf Ali
27.text: "Indeed we are shut out (of the fruits of our labour)!"

28.author: Ahmed Ali
28.text: One who was temperate among them, said: "Did I not say: 'Why don't you priase God?'"

28.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
28.text: The best among them said, “Did I not tell you, ‘Why do you not proclaim His purity?’”

28.author: Arberry
28.text: Said the most moderate of them, 'Did I not say to you, "Why do you not give glory?"'

28.author: Daryabadi
28.text: The most moderate of them said: said I not Unto you, wherefore hallow him ye not!

28.author: Hilali & Khan
28.text: The best among them said: "Did I not tell you: why do you not say: Insha' Allah (If Allah will)."

28.author: Itani
28.text: The most reasonable of them said, “Did I not say to you, ‘if only you would glorify?’”

28.author: Maududi
28.text: The best among them said: “Did I not say to you: why do you not give glory to (your Lord)?”

28.author: Mubarakpuri
28.text: The Awsat among them said; "Did I not tell you, `Why do you not Tusabbihun."'

28.author: Pickthall
28.text: The best among them said: Said I not unto you: Why glorify ye not (Allah)?

28.author: Qarai
28.text: The most upright among them said, ‘Did I not tell you, ‘‘Why do you not glorify [Allah]?’’ ’

28.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
28.text: (Whereupon) the best among them said: 'Did I not say to you to exalt (Allah)'

28.author: Saheeh International
28.text: The most moderate of them said, "Did I not say to you, 'Why do you not exalt [Allah]?' "

28.author: Sarwar
28.text: A reasonable one among them said, "Did I not tell you that you should glorify God?"

28.author: Shakir
28.text: The best of them said: Did I not say to you, Why do you not glorify (Allah)?

28.author: Wahiduddin Khan
28.text: The more upright of the two said, "Did I not bid you to glorify God?"

28.author: Yusuf Ali
28.text: Said one of them, more just (than the rest): "Did I not say to you, 'Why not glorify (Allah)?'"

28.author: Transliteration
28.text: Qala awsatuhum alam aqul lakum lawla tusabbihoona

29.author: Ahmed Ali
29.text: "Glory to our Lord," they said; we were really in the wrong."

29.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
29.text: They said, “Purity is to our Lord – we have indeed been unjust.”

29.author: Arberry
29.text: They said, 'Glory be to God, our Lord; truly, we were evildoers.'

29.author: Daryabadi
29.text: They said: hallowed be Our Lord! verily we have been wrong- doers.

29.author: Hilali & Khan
29.text: They said: "Glory to Our Lord! Verily, we have been Zalimun (wrong-doers, etc.)."

29.author: Itani
29.text: They said, “Glory to our Lord—We were indeed in the wrong.”

29.author: Maududi
29.text: They cried out: “Glory be to our Lord! Certainly we were sinners.”

29.author: Mubarakpuri
29.text: They said: "Glory to Our Lord! Verily, we have been wrongdoers."

29.author: Pickthall
29.text: They said: Glorified be our Lord! Lo! we have been wrong-doers.

29.author: Qarai
29.text: They said, ‘Immaculate is our Lord! We have indeed been wrongdoers!’

29.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
29.text: 'Exaltations to Allah, our Lord' they said, 'We were truly harmdoers'

29.author: Saheeh International
29.text: They said, "Exalted is our Lord! Indeed, we were wrongdoers."

29.author: Sarwar
29.text: They said, "All glory belongs to God. We have certainly been unjust".

29.author: Shakir
29.text: They said: Glory be to our Lord, surely we were unjust.

29.author: Wahiduddin Khan
29.text: They said, "Glory be to God, our Lord. We have surely done wrong."

29.author: Yusuf Ali
29.text: They said: "Glory to our Lord! Verily we have been doing wrong!"

29.author: Transliteration
29.text: Qaloo subhana rabbina inna kunna thalimeena

30.author: Ahmed Ali
30.text: Then they started blaming one another,

30.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
30.text: So they came towards each other, blaming.

30.author: Arberry
30.text: And they advanced one upon another, blaming each other.

30.author: Daryabadi
30.text: Then they turned to each other reproaching

30.author: Hilali & Khan
30.text: Then they turned, one against another, in blaming.

30.author: Itani
30.text: Then they turned to one another, blaming one another.

30.author: Maududi
30.text: Then they began to reproach one another.

30.author: Mubarakpuri
30.text: Then they turned one against another, blaming.

30.author: Pickthall
30.text: Then some of them drew near unto others, self-reproaching.

30.author: Qarai
30.text: Then they turned to one another, blaming each other.

30.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
30.text: And they came blaming one another.

30.author: Saheeh International
30.text: Then they approached one another, blaming each other.

30.author: Sarwar
30.text: Some of them started to blame others.

30.author: Shakir
30.text: Then some of them advanced against others, blaming each other.

30.author: Wahiduddin Khan
30.text: Then they began to heap reproaches on each other.

30.author: Yusuf Ali
30.text: Then they turned, one against another, in reproach.

31.author: Ahmed Ali
31.text: Saying: "Alas the woe, we were iniquitous.

31.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
31.text: They said, “Woe to us – we were indeed rebellious.”

31.author: Arberry
31.text: They said, 'Woe, alas for us! Truly, we were insolent.

31.author: Daryabadi
31.text: They said: woe Unto us! verily we! we have been arrogant.

31.author: Hilali & Khan
31.text: They said: "Woe to us! Verily, we were Taghun (transgressors and disobedient, etc.)

31.author: Itani
31.text: They said, “Woe to us—we were indeed domineering.

31.author: Maududi
31.text: They said: “Woe to us! We had indeed transgressed.

31.author: Mubarakpuri
31.text: They said: "Woe to us! Verily, we were Taghin."

31.author: Pickthall
31.text: They said: Alas for us! In truth we were outrageous.

31.author: Qarai
31.text: They said, ‘Woe to us! Indeed, we have been rebellious.

31.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
31.text: They said 'Woe to us, truly we were insolent'

31.author: Saheeh International
31.text: They said, "O woe to us; indeed we were transgressors.

31.author: Sarwar
31.text: They said, "Woe to us. We have been arrogant.

31.author: Shakir
31.text: Said they: O woe to us! surely we were inordinate:

31.author: Wahiduddin Khan
31.text: They said, "Alas for us, our behaviour was beyond the pale.

31.author: Yusuf Ali
31.text: They said: "Alas for us! We have indeed transgressed!

31.author: Transliteration
31.text: Qaloo ya waylana inna kunna tagheena

32.author: Ahmed Ali
32.text: Maybe our Lord will give us better than this. We turn to our Lord in supplication."

32.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
32.text: “Hopefully, our Lord will give us a better replacement than this – we now incline towards our Lord.”

32.author: Arberry
32.text: It may be that our Lord will give us in exchange a better than it; to our Lord we humbly turn.'

32.author: Daryabadi
32.text: Belike our Lord may exchange for us better garden than this; verily we are Unto our Lord beseechers.

32.author: Hilali & Khan
32.text: We hope that our Lord will give us in exchange a better (garden) than this. Truly, we turn to our Lord (wishing for good that He may forgive our sins, and reward us in the Hereafter)."

32.author: Itani
32.text: Perhaps our Lord will give us a better substitute for it. We are turning to our Lord.”

32.author: Maududi
32.text: Maybe our Lord will give us a better orchard in its place; to our Lord do we penitently turn.”

32.author: Mubarakpuri
32.text: "We hope that our Lord will give us in exchange better than it. Truly, we hope in our Lord."

32.author: Pickthall
32.text: It may be that our Lord will give us better than this in place thereof. Lo! we beseech our Lord.

32.author: Qarai
32.text: Maybe our Lord will give us a better one in its place. Indeed we earnestly beseech our Lord.’

32.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
32.text: It may be that our Lord will give us better than it in exchange. To our Lord we humbly turn'

32.author: Saheeh International
32.text: Perhaps our Lord will substitute for us [one] better than it. Indeed, we are toward our Lord desirous."

32.author: Sarwar
32.text: Perhaps our Lord will replace it with a better garden. We turn in repentance to our Lord".

32.author: Shakir
32.text: Maybe, our Lord will give us instead one better than it; surely to our Lord do we make our humble petition.

32.author: Wahiduddin Khan
32.text: May be our Lord will give us a better orchard in its stead; we turn to Him."

32.author: Yusuf Ali
32.text: "It may be that our Lord will give us in exchange a better (garden) than this: for we do turn to Him (in repentance)!"

32.author: Transliteration
32.text: AAasa rabbuna an yubdilana khayran minha inna ila rabbina raghiboona

33.author: Ahmed Ali
33.text: Such is Our chastisement; and the punishment of the Hereafter will be greater, if only they knew!

33.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
33.text: Such is the punishment; and indeed the punishment of the Hereafter is the greatest, if only they knew!

33.author: Arberry
33.text: Such is the chastisement; and the chastisement of the world to come, is assuredly greater, did they but know.

33.author: Daryabadi
33.text: Such is the torment. And the torment of the Hereafter is far greater; if they but knew.

33.author: Hilali & Khan
33.text: Such is the punishment (in this life), but truly, the punishment of the Hereafter is greater, if they but knew.

33.author: Itani
33.text: Such is the punishment; but the punishment of the Hereafter is greater, if they only knew.

33.author: Maududi
33.text: Such is the chastisement; and the chastisement of the Hereafter is assuredly even greater, if only they knew.

33.author: Mubarakpuri
33.text: Such is the punishment, but truly, the punishment of the Hereafter is greater if they but knew.

33.author: Pickthall
33.text: Such was the punishment. And verily the punishment of the Hereafter is greater if they did but know.

33.author: Qarai
33.text: Such was their punishment; and the punishment of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known.

33.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
33.text: Such was their punishment. But the punishment of the Everlasting Life is much greater, if they but knew.

33.author: Saheeh International
33.text: Such is the punishment [of this world]. And the punishment of the Hereafter is greater, if they only knew.

33.author: Sarwar
33.text: Such is the torment if only they knew that the torment in the life hereafter will certainly be greater.

33.author: Shakir
33.text: Such is the chastisement, and certainly the chastisement of the hereafter is greater, did they but know!

33.author: Wahiduddin Khan
33.text: Such was their punishment, [in this life]. But the punishment of the life to come is much more severe, if only they knew it!

33.author: Yusuf Ali
33.text: Such is the Punishment (in this life); but greater is the Punishment in the Hereafter,- if only they knew!

33.author: Transliteration
33.text: Kathalika alAAathabu walaAAathabu alakhirati akbaru law kanoo yaAAlamoona

34.author: Ahmed Ali
34.text: For those who fear God there are pleasure gardens with their Lord.

34.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
34.text: Indeed for the pious, with their Lord, are Gardens of Serenity.

34.author: Arberry
34.text: Surely for the godfearing shall be Gardens of Bliss with their Lord.

34.author: Daryabadi
34.text: Verily for the God-fearing, are with their Lord Gardens of Delight.

34.author: Hilali & Khan
34.text: Verily, for the Muttaqun (pious and righteous persons - see V. 2:2) are Gardens of delight (Paradise) with their Lord.

34.author: Itani
34.text: For the righteous are Gardens of Delight with their Lord.

34.author: Maududi
34.text: Surely the God-fearing shall have Gardens of bliss with their Lord.

34.author: Mubarakpuri
34.text: Verily, for those who have Taqwa are Gardens of Delight with their Lord.

34.author: Pickthall
34.text: Lo! for those who keep from evil are gardens of bliss with their Lord.

34.author: Qarai
34.text: Indeed for the Godwary there will be gardens of bliss near their Lord.

34.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
34.text: Surely, the cautious shall have Gardens of Bliss with their Lord.

34.author: Saheeh International
34.text: Indeed, for the righteous with their Lord are the Gardens of Pleasure.

34.author: Sarwar
34.text: The pious ones will receive a beautiful Paradise from their Lord.

34.author: Shakir
34.text: Surely those who guard (against evil) shall have with their Lord gardens of bliss.

34.author: Wahiduddin Khan
34.text: Those who are mindful of their Lord will be rewarded with gardens of bliss.

34.author: Yusuf Ali
34.text: Verily, for the Righteous, are Gardens of Delight, in the Presence of their Lord.

34.author: Transliteration
34.text: Inna lilmuttaqeena AAinda rabbihim jannati alnnaAAeemi

35.author: Ahmed Ali
35.text: Should We treat those who submit and obey in the same way as those who are culpable?

35.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
35.text: Shall We equate the Muslims to the guilty?

35.author: Arberry
35.text: What, shall we make those who have surrendered like to the sinners?

35.author: Daryabadi
35.text: Shall We then make the Muslims like the culprits?

35.author: Hilali & Khan
35.text: Shall We then treat the (submitting) Muslims like the Mujrimun (criminals, polytheists and disbelievers, etc.)?

35.author: Itani
35.text: Shall We treat the Muslims like the villains?

35.author: Maududi
35.text: What! Shall We treat those who have submitted (to Our command) like those who have acted as criminals?

35.author: Mubarakpuri
35.text: Shall We then treat the Muslims like the criminals

35.author: Pickthall
35.text: Shall We then treat those who have surrendered as We treat the guilty?

35.author: Qarai
35.text: Shall We treat those who submit [to Us] as [We treat] the guilty?

35.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
35.text: What, shall We make those who are submissive like the wrongdoers?

35.author: Saheeh International
35.text: Then will We treat the Muslims like the criminals?

35.author: Sarwar
35.text: Shall We treat the Muslims like criminals?

35.author: Shakir
35.text: What! shall We then make (that is, treat) those who submit as the guilty?

35.author: Wahiduddin Khan
35.text: Should We treat the true believers and the wrongdoers alike?

35.author: Yusuf Ali
35.text: Shall We then treat the People of Faith like the People of Sin?

36.author: Ahmed Ali
36.text: What has come upon you that you judge in such a wise?

36.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
36.text: What is the matter with you? What sort of a judgement you impose!

36.author: Arberry
36.text: What ails you then, how you judge?

36.author: Daryabadi
36.text: What aileth you? How ill ye judge!

36.author: Hilali & Khan
36.text: What is the matter with you? How judge you?

36.author: Itani
36.text: What is the matter with you? How do you judge?

36.author: Maududi
36.text: What is the matter with you? How ill do you judge!

36.author: Mubarakpuri
36.text: What is the matter with you How judge you

36.author: Pickthall
36.text: What aileth you? How foolishly ye judge!

36.author: Qarai
36.text: What is the matter with you? How do you judge!

36.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
36.text: What is the matter with you then, how do you judge?

36.author: Saheeh International
36.text: What is [the matter] with you? How do you judge?

36.author: Sarwar
36.text: What is the matter with you? How could you judge this to be so?

36.author: Shakir
36.text: What has happened to you? How do you judge?

36.author: Wahiduddin Khan
36.text: What ails you? How ill you judge!

36.author: Yusuf Ali
36.text: What is the matter with you? How judge ye?

37.author: Ahmed Ali
37.text: Or have you a Book in which you read

37.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
37.text: Is there a Book for you, from which you read?

37.author: Arberry
37.text: Or have you a Book wherein you study?

37.author: Daryabadi
37.text: Is there with you a Book wherein ye study:

37.author: Hilali & Khan
37.text: Or have you a Book through which you learn.

37.author: Itani
37.text: Or do you have a scripture in which you study.

37.author: Maududi
37.text: Or do you have a Book wherein you read

37.author: Mubarakpuri
37.text: Or have you a Book wherein you learn,

37.author: Pickthall
37.text: Or have ye a scripture wherein ye learn

37.author: Qarai
37.text: Do you possess a scripture in which you read

37.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
37.text: Or, have you a Book from which you study

37.author: Saheeh International
37.text: Or do you have a scripture in which you learn

37.author: Sarwar
37.text: Do you have a book from which you study

37.author: Shakir
37.text: Or have you a book wherein you read,

37.author: Wahiduddin Khan
37.text: Have you a Scripture that tells you

37.author: Yusuf Ali
37.text: Or have ye a book through which ye learn-

38.author: Ahmed Ali
38.text: That you can surely have whatever you choose?

38.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
38.text: - That for you in it is whatever you like?

38.author: Arberry
38.text: Surely therein you shall have whatever you choose!

38.author: Daryabadi
38.text: That therein is yours that which. ye may choose?

38.author: Hilali & Khan
38.text: That you shall have all that you choose?

38.author: Itani
38.text: Wherein there is whatever you choose?

38.author: Maududi
38.text: that (in the Hereafter) you shall have all that you choose for yourselves?

38.author: Mubarakpuri
38.text: That you shall therein have all that you choose

38.author: Pickthall
38.text: That ye shall indeed have all that ye choose?

38.author: Qarai
38.text: that you shall have in it whatever you choose?

38.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
38.text: surely in it you shall have whatever you choose!

38.author: Saheeh International
38.text: That indeed for you is whatever you choose?

38.author: Sarwar
38.text: that tells you to do whatever you want?

38.author: Shakir
38.text: That you have surely therein what you choose?

38.author: Wahiduddin Khan
38.text: that you will be granted whatever you choose?

38.author: Yusuf Ali
38.text: That ye shall have, through it whatever ye choose?

39.author: Ahmed Ali
39.text: Or have you taken a binding promise from Us which would hold till the Day of Judgement, that you will get whatever you demand?

39.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
39.text: Or is it that you have a covenant from Us, right up to the Day of Judgement, that you will get all what you claim?

39.author: Arberry
39.text: Or have you oaths from Us, reaching to the Day of Resurrection? Surely you shall have whatever you judge!

39.author: Daryabadi
39.text: Or have ye oaths from us reaching to the Day of Resurrection, that you shall be that which ye judge?

39.author: Hilali & Khan
39.text: Or you have oaths from Us, reaching to the Day of Resurrection that yours will be what you judge.

39.author: Itani
39.text: Or do you have oaths from Us, binding until the Day of Resurrection, that you will have whatever you demand?

39.author: Maududi
39.text: Or have We sworn a covenant with you which We are bound to keep till the Day of Resurrection, (a covenant requiring that whatever you ordain for yourselves shall be yours)?

39.author: Mubarakpuri
39.text: Or have you oaths from Us, lasting until the Day of Judgement, that yours will be what you judge

39.author: Pickthall
39.text: Or have ye a covenant on oath from Us that reacheth to the Day of Judgment, that yours shall be all that ye ordain?

39.author: Qarai
39.text: Do you have a pledge binding on Us until the Day of Resurrection, that you shall indeed have whatever you decide?

39.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
39.text: Or, have you an oath from Us that stretches to the Day of Resurrection (if so) you shall surely have what you judge!

39.author: Saheeh International
39.text: Or do you have oaths [binding] upon Us, extending until the Day of Resurrection, that indeed for you is whatever you judge?

39.author: Sarwar
39.text: Do you have a covenant with Us which allows you to do whatever you want until the Day of Judgment?

39.author: Shakir
39.text: Or have you received from Us an agreement confirmed by an oath extending to the day of resurrection that you shall surely have what you demand?

39.author: Wahiduddin Khan
39.text: Or do you have Our solemn oaths, binding upon Us till the Day of Resurrection, that you shall have whatever you yourselves decide?

39.author: Yusuf Ali
39.text: Or have ye Covenants with Us to oath, reaching to the Day of Judgment, (providing) that ye shall have whatever ye shall demand?

39.author: Transliteration
39.text: Am lakum aymanun AAalayna balighatun ila yawmi alqiyamati inna lakum lama tahkumoona

40.author: Ahmed Ali
40.text: Ask them: "Which of you is able to vouch for this?"

40.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
40.text: Ask them, who among them is a guarantor for it?

40.author: Arberry
40.text: Ask them, which of them will guarantee that!

40.author: Daryabadi
40.text: Ask then, which of them will stand thereof a surety?

40.author: Hilali & Khan
40.text: Ask them, which of them will stand surety for that!

40.author: Itani
40.text: Ask them, which of them will guarantee that.

40.author: Maududi
40.text: Ask them: “Which of them can guarantee that?

40.author: Mubarakpuri
40.text: Ask them, which of them will stand as surety for that!

40.author: Pickthall
40.text: Ask them (O Muhammad) which of them will vouch for that!

40.author: Qarai
40.text: Ask them, which of them will aver [any of] that!

40.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
40.text: Ask them, which of them will guarantee that!

40.author: Saheeh International
40.text: Ask them which of them, for that [claim], is responsible.

40.author: Sarwar
40.text: (Muhammad), ask which of them can guarantee that on the Day of Judgment.

40.author: Shakir
40.text: Ask them which of them will vouch for that,

40.author: Wahiduddin Khan
40.text: Ask them, which of them will vouch for that!

40.author: Yusuf Ali
40.text: Ask thou of them, which of them will stand surety for that!

41.author: Ahmed Ali
41.text: Or have they any partners? Let them bring their partners then, if what they say is true.

41.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
41.text: Or is it that they have partners in worship? So they should bring their appointed partners, if they are truthful.

41.author: Arberry
41.text: Or do they have associates? Then let them bring their associates, if they speak truly.

41.author: Daryabadi
41.text: Have they associate gods? Then let them produce their associate gods if they say sooth!

41.author: Hilali & Khan
41.text: Or have they "partners"? Then let them bring their "partners" if they are truthful!

41.author: Itani
41.text: Or do they have partners? Then let them produce their partners, if they are truthful.

41.author: Maududi
41.text: Or has something been guaranteed by any of those whom they associate with Allah in His Divinity?” If so, let them bring forth their associates, if they are truthful.

41.author: Mubarakpuri
41.text: Or have they "partners" Then let them bring their "partners" if they are truthful!

41.author: Pickthall
41.text: Or have they other gods? Then let them bring their other gods if they are truthful

41.author: Qarai
41.text: Do they have any ‘partners’ [that they claim for Allah]? Then let them produce their partners, if they are truthful.

41.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
41.text: Or, do they have associates? Then let them bring their associates, if what they say is true!

41.author: Saheeh International
41.text: Or do they have partners? Then let them bring their partners, if they should be truthful.

41.author: Sarwar
41.text: they will receive the same thing that the Muslims will? Do they have any witness to such an agreement? Let them bring out such witness, if they are truthful.

41.author: Shakir
41.text: Or have they associates if they are truthful.

41.author: Wahiduddin Khan
41.text: Or have they other partners [besides God]? Let them bring forth their other partners, if what they say be true.

41.author: Yusuf Ali
41.text: Or have they some "Partners" (in Allahhead)? Then let them produce their "partners", if they are truthful!

41.author: Transliteration
41.text: Am lahum shurakao falyatoo bishurakaihim in kanoo sadiqeena

42.author: Ahmed Ali
42.text: On the day the great calamity befalls, and they are called to bow in homage, they will not be able to do so.

42.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
42.text: On the day when the Shin* will be exposed and they will be called to prostrate themselves, they will be unable. (Used as a metaphor)

42.author: Arberry
42.text: Upon the day when the leg shall be bared, and they shall be summoned to bow themselves, but they cannot;

42.author: Daryabadi
42.text: Remember the Day whereon the shank shall be bared and they shall be called upon to Prostrate themselves, but they shall not be able.

42.author: Hilali & Khan
42.text: (Remember) the Day when the Shin shall be laid bare (i.e. the Day of Resurrection) and they shall be called to prostrate (to Allah), but they (hypocrites) shall not be able to do so,

42.author: Itani
42.text: On the Day when the Shin will be exposed, and they will be called to bow down, but they will be unable.

42.author: Maududi
42.text: On the Day when the dreadful calamity will unfold, when people will be summoned to prostrate themselves, and yet they will not be able to prostrate.

42.author: Mubarakpuri
42.text: The Day when the Shin shall be laid bare and they shall be called to prostrate themselves, but they shall not be able to do so.

42.author: Pickthall
42.text: On the day when it befalleth in earnest, and they are ordered to prostrate themselves but are not able,

42.author: Qarai
42.text: The day when the catastrophe occurs and they are summoned to prostrate themselves, they will not be able [to do it].

42.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
42.text: On the Day when the leg shall be bared, and they are ordered to prostrate themselves, they will be unable.

42.author: Saheeh International
42.text: The Day the shin will be uncovered and they are invited to prostration but the disbelievers will not be able,

42.author: Sarwar
42.text: On the day when the terrible torment approaches, they will be told (in a mocking way) to prostrate themselves, but they will not be able to do it.

42.author: Shakir
42.text: On the day when there shall be a severe affliction, and they shall be called upon to make obeisance, but they shall not be able,

42.author: Wahiduddin Khan
42.text: On the Day when the truth shall be laid bare, they will be called upon to prostrate themselves, but they will not be able to do so.

42.author: Yusuf Ali
42.text: The Day that the shin shall be laid bare, and they shall be summoned to bow in adoration, but they shall not be able,-

42.author: Transliteration
42.text: Yawma yukshafu AAan saqin wayudAAawna ila alssujoodi fala yastateeAAoona

43.author: Ahmed Ali
43.text: Lowered will be their eyes, disgrace overwhelming them. They had indeed been called to bow in homage when they were free of blame.

43.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
43.text: With lowered eyes, disgrace overcoming them; and indeed they used to be called to prostrate themselves whilst they were healthy.

43.author: Arberry
43.text: humbled shall be their eyes, and abasement shall overspread them, for they had been summoned to bow themselves while they were whole.

43.author: Daryabadi
43.text: Downcast will be their looks; abjectness will overspread them. Surely they had been called upon to Prostrate themselves while yet they were whole.

43.author: Hilali & Khan
43.text: Their eyes will be cast down, ignominy will cover them; they used to be called to prostrate (offer prayers), while they were healthy and good (in the life of the world, but they did not).

43.author: Itani
43.text: Their eyes subdued, shame will cover them. They were invited to bow down when they were sound.

43.author: Maududi
43.text: Their eyes shall be downcast and ignominy shall overwhelm them. For when they were safe and sound, they were summoned to prostrate themselves, (and they refused).

43.author: Mubarakpuri
43.text: Their eyes will be cast down and ignominy will cover them; they used to be called to prostrate themselves, while they were.

43.author: Pickthall
43.text: With eyes downcast, abasement stupefying them. And they had been summoned to prostrate themselves while they were yet unhurt.

43.author: Qarai
43.text: With a humbled look [in their eyes], they will be overcast by abasement. Certainly, they were summoned to prostrate themselves while they were yet sound.

43.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
43.text: Their eyes will be humbled, and humiliation shall spread over them for they were already ordered to prostrate themselves when they were undiminished.

43.author: Saheeh International
43.text: Their eyes humbled, humiliation will cover them. And they used to be invited to prostration while they were sound.

43.author: Sarwar
43.text: Their eyes will be lowered and disgrace will cover them. They had certainly been told to prostrate themselves before God when they were safe and sound.

43.author: Shakir
43.text: Their looks cast down, abasement shall overtake them; and they were called upon to make obeisance indeed while yet they were safe.

43.author: Wahiduddin Khan
43.text: Their eyes will be cast down and they will be covered in shame; they were bidden to prostrate themselves, when they were safe and sound [but they did not obey].

43.author: Yusuf Ali
43.text: Their eyes will be cast down,- ignominy will cover them; seeing that they had been summoned aforetime to bow in adoration, while they were whole, (and had refused).

43.author: Transliteration
43.text: KhashiAAatan absaruhum tarhaquhum thillatun waqad kanoo yudAAawna ila alssujoodi wahum salimoona

44.author: Ahmed Ali
44.text: So leave those who deny this Discourse to Me. We shall lead them step by step to (ruin) in a way they will not know.

44.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
44.text: Therefore leave the one who denies this matter, to Me; We shall soon steadily take them away, from a place they do not know.

44.author: Arberry
44.text: So leave Me with him who cries lies to this discourse! We will draw them on little by little whence they know not;

44.author: Daryabadi
44.text: Let Me alone with him who belieth this discourse; step by step We lead them on when they perceive not.

44.author: Hilali & Khan
44.text: Then leave Me Alone with such as belie this Quran. We shall punish them gradually from directions they perceive not.

44.author: Itani
44.text: So leave Me to those who reject this discourse; We will proceed against them gradually, from where they do not know.

44.author: Maududi
44.text: So leave Me, (O Prophet), to deal with him who gives the lie to this Discourse. We shall draw them little by little (to their undoing) in a way that they will not know.

44.author: Mubarakpuri
44.text: Then leave Me alone with whoever denies this narration. We shall punish them gradually from directions they perceive not.

44.author: Pickthall
44.text: Leave Me (to deal) with those who give the lie to this pronouncement. We shall lead them on by steps from whence they know not.

44.author: Qarai
44.text: So leave Me with those who deny this discourse. We will draw them imperceptibly [into ruin], whence they do not know.

44.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
44.text: Therefore, leave Me with he who belied this discourse. We will draw them on little by little from where they do not know.

44.author: Saheeh International
44.text: So leave Me, [O Muhammad], with [the matter of] whoever denies the Qur'an. We will progressively lead them [to punishment] from where they do not know.

44.author: Sarwar
44.text: Leave those who reject the Quran to Me and I shall lead them step by step to destruction, without their being aware of it.

44.author: Shakir
44.text: So leave Me and him who rejects this announcement; We will overtake them by degrees, from whence they perceive not:

44.author: Wahiduddin Khan
44.text: So leave to Me those who reject this message. We shall lead them step by step to their ruin, in ways beyond their ken.

44.author: Yusuf Ali
44.text: Then leave Me alone with such as reject this Message: by degrees shall We punish them from directions they perceive not.

44.author: Transliteration
44.text: Fatharnee waman yukaththibu bihatha alhadeethi sanastadrijuhum min haythu la yaAAlamoona

45.author: Ahmed Ali
45.text: Yet I will give them respite: Surely My plan is compact.

45.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
45.text: And I will give them respite; indeed My plan is very solid.

45.author: Arberry
45.text: and I shall respite them -- assuredly My guile is sure.

45.author: Daryabadi
45.text: And I bear with them; verily My contrivance is sure.

45.author: Hilali & Khan
45.text: And I will grant them a respite. Verily, My Plan is strong.

45.author: Itani
45.text: And I will give them respite. My plan is firm.

45.author: Maududi
45.text: I am giving them a respite. Great is My scheme!

45.author: Mubarakpuri
45.text: And I will grant them a respite. Verily, My plan is strong.

45.author: Pickthall
45.text: Yet I bear with them, for lo! My scheme is firm.

45.author: Qarai
45.text: I will grant them respite, for My devising is indeed sure.

45.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
45.text: I shall respite them, My stratagem is firm.

45.author: Saheeh International
45.text: And I will give them time. Indeed, My plan is firm.

45.author: Sarwar
45.text: I shall give them respite, however, My plan is so strong that they will never be able to escape from it.

45.author: Shakir
45.text: And I do bear with them, surely My plan is firm.

45.author: Wahiduddin Khan
45.text: I shall grant them some respite, for My plan is powerful.

45.author: Yusuf Ali
45.text: A (long) respite will I grant them: truly powerful is My Plan.

46.author: Ahmed Ali
46.text: Do you ask for any compensation from them that they are burdened with want?

46.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
46.text: Or is it that you (O dear Prophet Mohammed – peace and blessings be upon him) ask any fee from them, so they are burdened with the penalty?

46.author: Arberry
46.text: Or askest thou them for a wage, and so they are weighed down with debt?

46.author: Daryabadi
46.text: Askest thou a hire from them so that they are laden with debt?

46.author: Hilali & Khan
46.text: Or is it that you (O Muhammad SAW) ask them a wage, so that they are heavily burdened with debt?

46.author: Itani
46.text: Or do you ask them for a fee, so they are burdened with debt?

46.author: Maududi
46.text: Or are you asking them for some compensation so that they feel burdened with debt?

46.author: Mubarakpuri
46.text: Or is it that you ask them a wage, so that they are heavily burdened with debt

46.author: Pickthall
46.text: Or dost thou (Muhammad) ask a fee from them so that they are heavily taxed?

46.author: Qarai
46.text: Do you ask them for a reward, so that they are weighed down with debt?

46.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
46.text: Or, do you ask them for a wage, so that they are burdened withdebt?

46.author: Saheeh International
46.text: Or do you ask of them a payment, so they are by debt burdened down?

46.author: Sarwar
46.text: (Muhammad), do you ask for your preaching any recompense which is too heavy a price for them to pay?

46.author: Shakir
46.text: Or do you ask from them a reward, so that they are burdened with debt?

46.author: Wahiduddin Khan
46.text: Do you demand some recompense from them that would weigh them down with debt?

46.author: Yusuf Ali
46.text: Or is it that thou dost ask them for a reward, so that they are burdened with a load of debt?-

46.author: Transliteration
46.text: Am tasaluhum ajran fahum min maghramin muthqaloona

47.author: Ahmed Ali
47.text: Or do they have knowledge of the unknown which they copy down?

47.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
47.text: Or that they possess the hidden, so they are writing it?

47.author: Arberry
47.text: Or is the Unseen in their keeping, and so they are writing it down?

47.author: Daryabadi
47.text: Is with them the unseen, so that they write down?

47.author: Hilali & Khan
47.text: Or that the Ghaib (unseen here in this Verse it means Al-Lauh Al-Mahfuz) is in their hands, so that they can write it down?

47.author: Itani
47.text: Or do they know the future, and so they write it down?

47.author: Maududi
47.text: Or do they have any knowledge of the Unseen which they are now writing down?

47.author: Mubarakpuri
47.text: Or that the Unseen is in their hands, so that they can write it down

47.author: Pickthall
47.text: Or is the Unseen theirs that they can write (thereof)?

47.author: Qarai
47.text: Do they possess [access to] the Unseen, so that they write it down?

47.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
47.text: Or, is the Unseen with them, and they are writing it down!

47.author: Saheeh International
47.text: Or have they [knowledge of] the unseen, so they write [it] down?

47.author: Sarwar
47.text: Do they possess the knowledge of the unseen which confirms the truthfulness of their belief?

47.author: Shakir
47.text: Or have they (the knowledge of) the unseen, so that they write (it) down?

47.author: Wahiduddin Khan
47.text: Is the unseen within their grasp so that they write it down?

47.author: Yusuf Ali
47.text: Or that the Unseen is in their hands, so that they can write it down?

48.author: Ahmed Ali
48.text: So wait with patience for the judgement of your Lord, and do not be like (onah) of the fish who called (to his Lord) when he was choked with anger.

48.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
48.text: Therefore wait for your Lord’s command, and do not be like the one of the fish; who cried out when he was distraught. (Prophet Yunus – peace be upon him.)

48.author: Arberry
48.text: So be thou patient under the judgment of thy Lord, and be not as the Man of the Fish, when he called, choking inwardly.

48.author: Daryabadi
48.text: Be patient thou, then, with thy Lord's judgment, and be thou not like him of the fish, when he cried out while he was in anguish.

48.author: Hilali & Khan
48.text: So wait with patience for the Decision of your Lord, and be not like the Companion of the Fish, when he cried out (to Us) while he was in deep sorrow. (See the Quran, Verse 21:87).

48.author: Itani
48.text: So wait patiently for the Decision of your Lord, and do not be like the Fellow of the Fish who cried out in despair.

48.author: Maududi
48.text: So bear with patience until the Judgement of your Lord comes, and do not belike the man in the fish (i.e., Jonah) who called out, choking with grief:

48.author: Mubarakpuri
48.text: So, wait with patience for the decision of your Lord, and be not like the Companion of the Fish when he cried out (to Us) while he was Makzum.

48.author: Pickthall
48.text: But wait thou for thy Lord's decree, and be not like him of the fish, who cried out in despair.

48.author: Qarai
48.text: So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord, and do not be like the Man of the Fish who called out as he choked with grief.

48.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
48.text: So be patient with the judgement of your Lord and do not be like the Companion of the Whale (Jonah), when he called out choking inwardly.

48.author: Saheeh International
48.text: Then be patient for the decision of your Lord, [O Muhammad], and be not like the companion of the fish when he called out while he was distressed.

48.author: Sarwar
48.text: Exercise patience until the promise of your Lord (to punish the unbelievers) comes true. Do not be like Jonah (who left his people without the permission of God; he wanted them to be punished immediately),

48.author: Shakir
48.text: So wait patiently for the judgment of your Lord, and be not like the companion of the fish, when he cried while he was in distress.

48.author: Wahiduddin Khan
48.text: Wait patiently for your Lord's judgement; do not be like the man who, having been swallowed by a whale, called out in distress.

48.author: Yusuf Ali
48.text: So wait with patience for the Command of thy Lord, and be not like the Companion of the Fish,- when he cried out in agony.

48.author: Transliteration
48.text: Faisbir lihukmi rabbika wala takun kasahibi alhooti ith nada wahuwa makthoomun

49.author: Ahmed Ali
49.text: Had it not been for a favour from his Lord he would have been cast blame-worthy on a barren plain.

49.author: Ahmed Raza Khan
49.text: Were it not for his Lord’s favour that reached him, he would have surely been cast onto the desolate land, reproached.

49.author: Arberry
49.text: Had there not overtaken him a blessing from his Lord he would have been cast upon the wilderness, being condemned.

49.author: Daryabadi
49.text: Had not grace from his Lord reached him, he had surely been cast into the wilderness in a plight.

49.author: Hilali & Khan
49.text: Had not a Grace from his Lord reached him, he would indeed have been (left in the stomach of the fish, but We forgave him), so he was cast off on the naked shore, while he was to be blamed.

49.author: Itani
49.text: Were it not for his Lord’s favor that reached him, he would have been thrown into the wilderness, fully despised.

49.author: Maududi
49.text: had his Lord not bestowed His favour upon him, he would have been cast upon that barren shore (and would have remained there) in disgrace.

49.author: Mubarakpuri
49.text: Had not a grace from his Lord reached him, he would indeed have been (left in the stomach of the fish, but We forgave him), so he was cast off on the naked shore, while he was to be blamed.

49.author: Pickthall
49.text: Had it not been that favour from his Lord had reached him he surely had been cast into the wilderness while he was reprobate.

49.author: Qarai
49.text: Had it not been for a blessing that came to his rescue from his Lord, he would surely have been cast on the bare shore, being blameworthy.

49.author: Qaribullah & Darwish
49.text: Had the favor of his Lord not come upon him, he would have been blamed, cast upon the shore.

49.author: Saheeh International
49.text: If not that a favor from his Lord overtook him, he would have been thrown onto the naked shore while he was censured.

49.author: Sarwar
49.text: and who cried (for help) to his Lord, while imprisoned and helpless inside the fish. Had it not been for a favor from his Lord, he would have been left out in the open, deserving blame for his shortcomings.

49.author: Shakir
49.text: Were it not that favor from his Lord had overtaken him, he would certainly have been cast down upon the naked Found while he was blamed.

49.author: Wahiduddin Khan
49.text: Had his Lord's grace not been bestowed upon him, he would have been cast away in disgrace upon that desolate shore.

49.author: Yusuf Ali
